---
description: "Use when reviewing or editing blog posts, articles, or long-form markdown content. Default to a devil's-advocate review of topic strength, correctness of claims, and concrete suggestions to improve while preserving the author's tone. Expand abbreviations on first use using Capital Case, e.g. Artificial Intelligence (AI)."
name: 'Article Review Preferences'
applyTo: 'src/pages/blog/**/*.md'
---

# Article Review Preferences

- Default to a devil's-advocate lens for article and blog reviews.
- Focus on overall topic strength, correctness of claims, and concrete suggestions to improve.
- Prefer content critique over tone critique unless tone is explicitly requested.
- Preserve the author's existing tone when suggesting edits; strengthen content without making the voice more formal.
- Expand abbreviations on first use using Capital Case for the full phrase, for example Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Individual Contributors (ICs).
- If asked to apply suggestions, prefer editing the file directly rather than only describing them.
- Each paragraph should cover exactly one topic or idea. If a paragraph shifts to a new point mid-way, split it. Avoid merging distinct thoughts into one block just to reduce paragraph count.
- Avoid overusing em dashes (—). Alternate between em dashes and semicolons so no two consecutive uses of the em dash appear in the same section. Prefer a semicolon when joining two closely related independent clauses. Em dashes must not be surrounded by spaces: write "a—b", not "a — b".
- Avoid AI-like paragraph patterns: do not write paragraphs that are only one or two sentences long and end on a repetitive punchline or summary restatement. Paragraphs should develop a point with enough substance to stand on their own, but should not become walls of text by absorbing adjacent topics.
- Do not use a semicolon before a subordinate clause (e.g. "though", "although", "while"). Semicolons join independent clauses; use a comma for subordinate qualifiers.
- Avoid vague spatial metaphors like "in the middle" or "somewhere between". Prefer precise phrasing that names what the position actually means, e.g. "doing both" or "taking on responsibilities from both tracks".
- Keep contraction style consistent within an article. If the surrounding prose uses spelled-out forms ("That is", "It is"), avoid mixing in contractions ("That's", "It's") in the same piece.
